text.title: api-monitoring-alerting Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for api-monitoring-alerting as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Writing alert rules before defining SLOs.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Thresholds without SLO anchors are arbitrary. They generate constant false positives that desensitize on-call to real incidents. Define what "good" means (SLO), then derive thresholds from the error budget. Always require PM/ops sign-off on SLO targets — they are business commitments.

failure-mode.description: Using simple threshold alerts ("error rate > 1% for 5m") as the primary SLO protection.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Single-window threshold alerts fire on transient spikes (deploy, cold-start, downstream blip) that do not actually threaten the monthly SLO. They also miss slow burns that erode the error budget over days without triggering any alert. Multi-window burn-rate catches both modes. Reference: SRE Workbook ch.5.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: An on-call engineer woken at 3am without a runbook has to reconstruct debugging steps from memory under pressure. Every alert that is worth waking someone for is worth writing a runbook for. Block ruleset commits without runbook_url in CI.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Hand-edited JSON produces formatting inconsistencies that generate large diffs and conflicts. Manage dashboards as code via jsonnet or grafanactl pull; commit only machine-formatted JSON. This also enables PR review of dashboard changes and rollback via git revert.
